column2: The EIS evaluates EPA construction grant funding for the Phase I conveyance tunnels of the Tunnel and Reservoir Plan (TARP), proposed by the Metropolitan Sanitary District of Greater Chicago, with focus on the Lower Des Plaines Tunnel System. The Des Plaines component includes about 26 miles of tunnel located 150-325 feet underground in dolomite, 55 drop shafts, 10 access shafts, 5 construction shafts, 80 collecting structures, and one pumping station, all designed to capture combined-sewer overflows and convey them to treatment plants. The Des Plaines system alone is estimated at $346.9 mill…

column2: The MSDGC service area covers about 860 square miles in Cook County, Illinois, including Chicago and roughly 120 municipalities with a population near 5.5 million. The Des Plaines tunnel route passes through highly urbanized, mostly residential watersheds and Cook County Forest Preserve lands, serving suburbs such as Des Plaines, Park Ridge, Maywood, Riverside, Brookfield, and Forest Park. The area has experienced higher-than-national unemployment (about 9.6% in Cook County and 11.2% in Chicago in 1975), and households face potential property tax increases tied to the project.

The Flood Control Coordinating Committee developed 23 alternative plans over two decades, eliminated 6, and modified the remaining 17 into 51 subplans evaluated against eight parameters including cost, benefits, and construction impacts. Four favorable Chicago Underflow plans were consolidated into TARP, which ranked highest against alternatives (including no-action) on 15 environmental criteria as the least costly and most environmentally acceptable option. If TARP were not built, alternatives such as instream aeration, treatment plant expansion, or reallocation of about $300 million in fede…

The tunnels would capture about 90 percent of combined-sewer overflow pollutant load and reduce overflow frequency from roughly 100 to 10 times per year, but Illinois water quality standards for dissolved oxygen and ammonia would not be fully met without additional treatment upgrades, reservoirs, and instream aeration. Construction will generate about 3.78 million cubic yards of mostly non-marketable rock spoil, cause localized noise, dust, vibration, traffic disruption, and glare, and pose worker safety risks (an estimated 84 disabling injuries for the Des Plaines system). MSDGC held public meetings in October 1972 and July 1973 on the TARP plan and its environmental issues, and additional hearings were conducted by the Northern Illinois Planning Commission (1974), MSDGC (July 1975), Illinois EPA (September 1975), and the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers (November 1975).
